sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage:
Researchers investigated a spring-loaded prosthetic ankle element compressed by different loads while investigators measured displacement and stored elastic energy to connect a measurable physical trend with a the physical sciences principle. The central idea was that for an ideal spring, restoring force is proportional to displacement and the stored elastic potential energy grows with the square of displacement. Before collecting data, the team identified the governing relationship as F = kx and Us = 1/2 kx^2. This mattered because the same topic vocabulary could support several tempting but incompatible answers unless the variables, units, and controlled conditions were kept straight. The investigators therefore designed the experiment so that the figure had to be interpreted through proportionality, limiting behavior, and the direction of causality rather than through sentence recall alone.

The experimental manipulation involved compressing the spring by increasing amounts while keeping the same spring and alignment geometry. Figure 1 summarizes the main result: force increased linearly with displacement, while calculated stored energy increased more steeply. The investigators repeated the measurements after verifying that spring material, loading direction, temperature, and displacement calibration were held constant. Those controls were important because a change in a calibration setting, environmental condition, sample geometry, or hidden concentration could create an apparent trend without testing the intended physical relationship. A correct explanation must therefore account for both what was deliberately changed and what was explicitly held constant.

Two alternative interpretations were considered. One was that doubling displacement doubles stored energy because all mechanical quantities scale linearly. Another was that a spring stores no energy unless the user performs net horizontal work. Each alternative contains a familiar phrase from chemistry or physics, so it could attract a student who recognizes a term but does not check whether the prediction matches the graph. The investigators rejected these explanations because they either reverse the expected direction, treat a nonlinear relationship as linear, ignore a conserved quantity, or attribute the effect to a controlled variable.

The follow-up analysis asked students to make a prediction from the same relationship. For this passage, the key is to connect the measured trend to spring-loaded prosthetic joint, translate the graph into the appropriate mathematical dependency, and use the controls to rule out artifacts. The relevant calculation is intentionally modest; the harder part is deciding which variable belongs in the numerator, denominator, exponent, logarithm, or proportionality.
questions.stem: Which interpretation best explains the main result in the spring-loaded prosthetic joint experiment?

choices.A: Doubling displacement doubles stored energy because all mechanical quantities scale linearly
choices.B: The result is best explained by Hooke-law behavior with energy accumulating as the area under the force-displacement curve
choices.C: A spring stores no energy unless the user performs net horizontal work, even though the passage states that spring material was controlled and the measured trend moved systematically in the figure
choices.D: The pattern is most likely random noise despite the systematic dose-response trend and stated controls

explanations.A: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design. The claim that Doubling displacement doubles stored energy because all mechanical quantities scale linearly conflicts with the stated relationship, F = kx and Us = 1/2 kx^2, or with the direction shown in the figure. The passage reports that force increased linearly with displacement, while calculated stored energy increased more steeply and states that spring material, loading direction, temperature, and displacement calibration were held constant. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.
explanations.B: Correct. This answer accounts for the manipulation, the plotted trend, and the controlled variables at the same time. The passage identifies the relevant relationship as F = kx and Us = 1/2 kx^2, so the reasoning depends on how the variables are related rather than on a keyword match. In this experiment, force increased linearly with displacement, while calculated stored energy increased more steeply, while spring material, loading direction, temperature, and displacement calibration were held constant. That pattern supports this choice because The result is best explained by Hooke-law behavior with energy accumulating as the area under the force-displacement curve. It also predicts the correct follow-up behavior if the same variable is changed again. The tempting alternatives either reverse the dependency, mistake a controlled variable for the causal variable, treat a nonlinear relationship as linear, or confuse a measured signal with the physical quantity that produced it.
explanations.C: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design. The claim that A spring stores no energy unless the user performs net horizontal work, even though the passage states that spring material was controlled and the measured trend moved systematically in the figure conflicts with the stated relationship, F = kx and Us = 1/2 kx^2, or with the direction shown in the figure. The passage reports that force increased linearly with displacement, while calculated stored energy increased more steeply and states that spring material, loading direction, temperature, and displacement calibration were held constant. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.
explanations.D: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design. The claim that The pattern is most likely random noise despite the systematic dose-response trend and stated controls conflicts with the stated relationship, F = kx and Us = 1/2 kx^2, or with the direction shown in the figure. The passage reports that force increased linearly with displacement, while calculated stored energy increased more steeply and states that spring material, loading direction, temperature, and displacement calibration were held constant. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.

questions.stem: Which observation from the figure most directly supports the proposed explanation?

choices.A: The graph shows no systematic change when compressing the spring by increasing amounts while keeping the same spring and alignment geometry
choices.B: The measured response changes only after a controlled variable is deliberately altered
choices.C: The figure shows force rising in proportion to displacement instead of remaining constant or dropping with compression
choices.D: The plotted trend is opposite to the direction predicted by the stated relationship

questions.stem: Which alternative explanation is most directly weakened by the controls in the passage?

choices.A: The passage still supports the idea that for an ideal spring, restoring force is proportional to displacement and the stored elastic potential energy grows with the square of displacement
choices.B: The response changes in the direction expected from the stated manipulation
choices.C: The constant spring material and alignment weaken the claim that the larger force came from a different effective spring rather than greater compression
choices.D: The graph can be interpreted only by comparing the measured trend with the governing equation

questions.topic: Hooke law and elastic potential energy

questions.stem: Which broader Chemical and Physical Foundations principle is most directly illustrated by this experiment?

choices.A: Quantitative graphs should be interpreted without considering units or controlled variables
choices.B: A controlled variable is the most likely cause of a trend whenever it appears in an equation
choices.C: Elastic systems convert external work into recoverable potential energy according to force-displacement relationships
choices.D: A memorized definition should override a measured trend when the two appear inconsistent, even though the passage states that spring material was controlled and the measured trend moved systematically in the figure

questions.topic: Hooke law and elastic potential energy

questions.passage:
Researchers investigated a rehabilitation treadmill that raised patients through the same vertical height using ramps of different length to connect a measurable physical trend with a the physical sciences principle. The central idea was that ideal mechanical advantage can reduce required force by increasing distance, but it does not eliminate the work needed to gain gravitational potential energy. Before collecting data, the team identified the governing relationship as W = Fd and ΔUg = mgh. This mattered because the same topic vocabulary could support several tempting but incompatible answers unless the variables, units, and controlled conditions were kept straight. The investigators therefore designed the experiment so that the figure had to be interpreted through proportionality, limiting behavior, and the direction of causality rather than through sentence recall alone.

The experimental manipulation involved comparing the average pulling force needed to move the same load up short, medium, and long inclines to the same height. Figure 1 summarizes the main result: average force decreased as ramp length increased, but calculated work against gravity remained approximately constant. The investigators repeated the measurements after verifying that load mass, vertical rise, surface material, and starting speed were controlled. Those controls were important because a change in a calibration setting, environmental condition, sample geometry, or hidden concentration could create an apparent trend without testing the intended physical relationship. A correct explanation must therefore account for both what was deliberately changed and what was explicitly held constant.

Two alternative interpretations were considered. One was that a longer ramp reduces the required force because the object gains less gravitational potential energy. Another was that mechanical advantage creates extra useful energy so total work output exceeds work input. Each alternative contains a familiar phrase from chemistry or physics, so it could attract a student who recognizes a term but does not check whether the prediction matches the graph. The investigators rejected these explanations because they either reverse the expected direction, treat a nonlinear relationship as linear, ignore a conserved quantity, or attribute the effect to a controlled variable.

The follow-up analysis asked students to make a prediction from the same relationship. For this passage, the key is to connect the measured trend to inclined treadmill work test, translate the graph into the appropriate mathematical dependency, and use the controls to rule out artifacts. The relevant calculation is intentionally modest; the harder part is deciding which variable belongs in the numerator, denominator, exponent, logarithm, or proportionality.
questions.stem: Which interpretation best explains the main result in the inclined treadmill work test experiment?

choices.A: The lower force is best explained by spreading a similar gravitational work requirement over a longer path
choices.B: A longer ramp reduces the required force because the object gains less gravitational potential energy
choices.C: Mechanical advantage creates extra useful energy so total work output exceeds work input, even though the passage states that load mass was controlled and the measured trend moved systematically in the figure
choices.D: The pattern is most likely random noise despite the systematic dose-response trend and stated controls

explanations.A: Correct. This answer accounts for the manipulation, the plotted trend, and the controlled variables at the same time. The passage identifies the relevant relationship as W = Fd and ΔUg = mgh, so the reasoning depends on how the variables are related rather than on a keyword match. In this experiment, average force decreased as ramp length increased, but calculated work against gravity remained approximately constant, while load mass, vertical rise, surface material, and starting speed were controlled. That pattern supports this choice because The lower force is best explained by spreading a similar gravitational work requirement over a longer path. It also predicts the correct follow-up behavior if the same variable is changed again. The tempting alternatives either reverse the dependency, mistake a controlled variable for the causal variable, treat a nonlinear relationship as linear, or confuse a measured signal with the physical quantity that produced it.
explanations.B: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design. The claim that A longer ramp reduces the required force because the object gains less gravitational potential energy conflicts with the stated relationship, W = Fd and ΔUg = mgh, or with the direction shown in the figure. The passage reports that average force decreased as ramp length increased, but calculated work against gravity remained approximately constant and states that load mass, vertical rise, surface material, and starting speed were controlled. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.
explanations.C: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design. The claim that Mechanical advantage creates extra useful energy so total work output exceeds work input, even though the passage states that load mass was controlled and the measured trend moved systematically in the figure conflicts with the stated relationship, W = Fd and ΔUg = mgh, or with the direction shown in the figure. The passage reports that average force decreased as ramp length increased, but calculated work against gravity remained approximately constant and states that load mass, vertical rise, surface material, and starting speed were controlled. The passage reports that average force decreased as ramp length increased, but calculated work against gravity remained approximately constant and states that load mass, vertical rise, surface material, and starting speed were controlled. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.

choices.A: The passage still supports the idea that ideal mechanical advantage can reduce required force by increasing distance, but it does not eliminate the work needed to gain gravitational potential energy
choices.B: The fixed vertical rise and mass weaken the claim that the force drop occurred because the load gained less gravitational potential energy
choices.C: The response changes in the direction expected from the stated manipulation
choices.D: The graph can be interpreted only by comparing the measured trend with the governing equation

explanations.A: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design. The claim that The passage still supports the idea that ideal mechanical advantage can reduce required force by increasing distance, but it does not eliminate the work needed to gain gravitational potential energy conflicts with the stated relationship, W = Fd and ΔUg = mgh, or with the direction shown in the figure. The passage reports that average force decreased as ramp length increased, but calculated work against gravity remained approximately constant and states that load mass, vertical rise, surface material, and starting speed were controlled. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.
explanations.B: Correct. This answer accounts for the manipulation, the plotted trend, and the controlled variables at the same time. The passage identifies the relevant relationship as W = Fd and ΔUg = mgh, so the reasoning depends on how the variables are related rather than on a keyword match. In this experiment, average force decreased as ramp length increased, but calculated work against gravity remained approximately constant, while load mass, vertical rise, surface material, and starting speed were controlled. That pattern supports this choice because The fixed vertical rise and mass weaken the claim that the force drop occurred because the load gained less gravitational potential energy. It also predicts the correct follow-up behavior if the same variable is changed again. The tempting alternatives either reverse the dependency, mistake a controlled variable for the causal variable, treat a nonlinear relationship as linear, or confuse a measured signal with the physical quantity that produced it.
explanations.C: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.
explanations.D: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design. The claim that The graph can be interpreted only by comparing the measured trend with the governing equation conflicts with the stated relationship, W = Fd and ΔUg = mgh, or with the direction shown in the figure. The passage reports that average force decreased as ramp length increased, but calculated work against gravity remained approximately constant and states that load mass, vertical rise, surface material, and starting speed were controlled. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.

questions.stem: Which broader Chemical and Physical Foundations principle is most directly illustrated by this experiment?

choices.A: Simple machines trade force for distance while respecting conservation of energy
choices.B: Quantitative graphs should be interpreted without considering units or controlled variables
choices.C: A controlled variable is the most likely cause of a trend whenever it appears in an equation
choices.D: A memorized definition should override a measured trend when the two appear inconsistent
